title: Visa Inc. (V) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Visa Inc. (V) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: 3.5% reflects Visa's enduring economic moat. As an inflation-protected asset whose fortunes are tied to global consumer spending, Visa will sustainably grow at a rate equal to or slightly exceeding long-term global nominal GDP growth. Their pricing power ensures terminal cash flows remain robust indefinitely.

thesisTitle: The AI Thesis: Why Visa's Moat is Indestructible
thesisParagraphs: Visa operates the world's premier electronic payments network, effectively functioning as a global tollbooth on consumer spending. Despite macroeconomic headwinds and inflationary pressures, Visa's business model is inherently insulated: as prices rise, so do nominal transaction values, translating directly to revenue growth with minimal incremental capital expenditure.

While skeptics point to regulatory pressure and the rise of alternative payment networks, my analysis reveals a different truth. The sheer scale of Visa's two-sided network—billions of cards and millions of merchants—creates a nearly insurmountable barrier to entry. Furthermore, their strategic pivot towards high-margin Value-Added Services (VAS), such as fraud management and data analytics, provides a massive runway for sustained double-digit free cash flow compounding. Visa is not just a payment rail; it's a global financial operating system.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 18, 2026).

faq.question: Why did Gemini pick an 11% growth rate for Visa?
faq.answer: Visa's core payments network acts as a global tollbooth. While consumer spending growth is stable, their expansion into B2B payments, cross-border transactions, and high-margin Value-Added Services (VAS) supports an 11% compound annual FCF growth over the next five years.

faq.question: What discount rate was used for Visa's DCF?
faq.answer: An 8.5% discount rate was used. This relatively low WACC reflects Visa's extraordinarily strong business model, high operating margins (often exceeding 65%), and low revenue volatility compared to the broader market, offsetting the current 4.18% risk-free rate.

faq.question: What are the biggest risks to Visa's intrinsic value?
faq.answer: The primary risks include antitrust regulations (such as the Credit Card Competition Act), disintermediation by alternative payment networks (like FedNow or digital wallets bypassing traditional rails), and global economic recessions dampening total payment volume.
